Wrecked on my first CS match! From internet café to understanding IPs
My first time at an internet café

In the previous post I mentioned that after my computer broke, I started going to internet cafés. But actually, before I had a computer, I was already going to cafés often — and there's a story behind that. Let me tell you about my first internet café experience.
After elementary school every day, I'd go play basketball (I was super into it back then). One day, a classmate came over and said...
"Hey! Ray! Are you going to play basketball today as usual?" my classmate asked.
"Yeah, but I have to go home and drop off my backpack first." I answered.
"Oh, want to come to a great place with me instead?" Looking back, the way my classmate said "great place" sounded like he was about to take me somewhere shady.
"Huh? Where?" My after-school routine was: home → drop off backpack → school basketball court. So this "great place" was very curious to me.
"Just come with me, you got NT$10?" my classmate asked.
"Yeah!" I had, this was the era when NT$50 was still issued as paper money — though that didn't last long either.
"Let's go!" he said. We walked maybe 5–8 minutes from the school gate, ending up at a place I'd never been before.

It was a building tucked in a back alley. The glass door was covered with stickers so you couldn't see inside. When the door opened, I stepped into a whole new world — dim lighting, loud noise, rows of computers all occupied, every screen showing some game.
Back then, a famous café was called "Blue Language Life". Cafés weren't chains like today — most were small individual shops. I learned later that the one we went to was actually unlicensed.
What stuck with me was that almost everyone there was gaming — Ragnarok Online, Lineage, CS, etc. The vibe was best described in one word:
Whenever someone lost, got killed, or had a useless teammate, you'd hear shouting and cursing. Common scene.
My classmate found two open seats and pulled me in.
"Take out your NT$10 and drop it in that coin slot — then you can use the computer!" he pointed at a coin machine.
I dropped in NT$10, the screen on the slot showed a number (minutes), and the computer booted into Windows desktop. (NT$10 bought you 30–60 minutes back then, depending on the shop.)

Two payment types: coin-operated and full-rent. Coin-op = you drop money for time. Full-rent = you book the seat for a few hours or a whole day. Full-rent was usually cheaper. Some chains even sold food — hot-pot noodles, braised pork rice. A lot of cafés had really good hot-pot noodles.
Once we'd settled in, my classmate opened a game for me — CS (Counter-Strike). A classic FPS.
"Ray, you know how to play CS?" my classmate asked while opening the game.
"No, what is it?" I stared at his screen, confused.
"It's a shooter! I'll give you a number — click here to enter CS, find LAN, type in my number, then I'll teach you..." he rambled.
You can guess how this went. I got completely wrecked because I'd never touched it. Years later I realized that string of numbers was the "IP" — used to let our computers connect to each other.
Before we get into IPs, let's talk about "Local Area Network" (LAN). LAN is closer to your daily life than you think. If your home has internet, you already have a LAN.
LAN = Local Area Network. In short: a network that lets multiple computers within a limited area connect to each other. Think "internal network". Common in homes, schools, offices.
File sharing, shared folders, shared printers
Private — fits a single org or individual
High throughput, not limited by external network speed
Everything funnels through a central device — usually a router or switch — which connects to other devices: computers, printers, etc.
Not all printers connect to LAN directly. Some need USB to a computer first, then sharing through the computer. Depends on the model. Printers that support LAN directly are called
— they have a built-in network interface.
"What's the difference between LAN and the internet?"
This is where the important concept comes in — "external network" vs "internal network".
LAN is a small "internal" network. The internet is the global "external" network. LAN can connect to the internet, but the internet can't necessarily connect to your LAN.
So when people say "external network" or "internal network":
Are there sub-categories of LAN? Yes — as the area grows, from small to large:
multiple LANs across a campus or large company.
cross-city, cross-country networks — aka external network.
This might be a bit abstract — let me illustrate WAN with an example.

Once upon a time, there was Xiaoming who lived in Taipei. He had three computers and a network printer at home, all connected to the internet via one router. That router-based setup at Xiaoming's home is a LAN.
Then Xiaoming uses that router to reach the internet, and can chat / watch videos / game with Xiaomei in Tainan.
That's a simplified illustration of LAN and WAN. Real-world architecture is way more complex, but this should give you a baseline understanding.
If you find it hard to differentiate, just know: external network =

In everyday or teaching context, "external = WAN, internal = LAN" is the typical shorthand.
WAN doesn't necessarily equal Internet — enterprises can have their own private WAN that doesn't touch the public internet, e.g., military networks.
What's an IP and why do we need it?
IP = Internet Protocol. It's a number string that identifies each device on a network — like an address. To deliver a letter, the postman needs an address. Network devices work the same way.
On an internal network, lots of devices might be connected: computers, printers, routers. How do you tell them apart on the network?
Internal-network IPs are usually assigned by the router, typically starting with 192.168, e.g.:
These numbers identify each device within the LAN.

Try opening your browser and going to 192.168.0.1 — this is most routers' default IP. You'd land in the router's admin panel, but you need the username and password. If you're not familiar with router settings, don't change anything to avoid network issues.

Note: these IPs can't be used directly on the internet — they're reserved for internal networks. To reach the external network, you go through a public IP. Public IPs touch on more concepts — we'll skip them for now to stay focused on IP basics.
A single IP has four number groups, each 0–255. So IP range goes from 0.0.0.0 to 255.255.255.255. This format is IPv4.

IPv4 is the most common and easiest to remember. But as devices proliferate (phones, TVs, fridges, washing machines, etc.), IPv4 isn't enough anymore. IP exhaustion can cause IP collisions, making things unable to reach the internet. That's why IPv6 exists.
IPv4 vs IPv6 difference? IPv4 has 2^32 = 4,294,967,296 addresses. IPv6 has 2^128 = 340,282,366,841,710,000,000,000,000,000,000 addresses. Enough for every grain of sand on Earth to have its own IP.
IPv6 has a special format — not just plain decimal groups. It's 8 groups of hex, each 0–FFFF:
You don't need to memorize this — these are for computers. For us, understanding the concept of IP is enough.
IPs are most common in school networks, enterprise networks, or cloud services (GCP, AWS, Azure). Home networks can have them too, but home is mostly
as another flavor of internal IP.
In short, for us regular humans, no need to memorize the weird long strings. Just know IP is what lets devices around the world find each other.
When was your first "get completely wrecked" gaming experience?
Any CS veterans remember the B+3+1 shortcut? I still remember it — that was the buy-an-SMG combo (lol).
